channel.title: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open - Latest Articles

image.title: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open - Latest Articles

item.author: Chen, Dawei; Yan, Xin; Hu, Lingling
item.title: Autologous Tooth Bone Powder: A Review of Osteogenic Mechanism, Preparation, and Clinical Application
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Mon, 18 May 2026 00:00:00 GMT-05:00

Alveolar bone resorption is a common complication after tooth extraction, leading to insufficient bone quality and volume that impairs subsequent implant restoration. To address this issue, bone graft materials have become an essential therapeutic option. As a bone graft material derived from the patient’s own teeth, autologous tooth bone powder has emerged as a research hotspot in the field due to its unique advantages, including convenient harvesting and excellent biocompatibility. This review summarizes the current research status of autologous teeth in terms of osteogenic basis, preparation methods, and clinical applications, aiming to provide clear theoretical guidance for clinical practice.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 4(2):e0059, June 2026. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000059

item.author: Lian, Wenli; Wang, Min
item.title: Imaging Features and Clinical Diagnosis and Treatment Analysis of Endolymphatic Sac Tumor in the Temporal Bone: A Case Report and Literature Review
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Thu, 14 May 2026 00:00:00 GMT-05:00

Objectives: Share clinical experience in the diagnosis and treatment of endolymphatic sac tumor (ELST). Case Report: This article reported a case of a 53-year-old female patient with ELST and described her imaging features, surgical findings, and pathologic characteristics. A mass was found in the petrous portion of the left temporal bone with surrounding bone destruction on computed tomography (CT). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a heterogeneous lesion in the left cerebellopontine angle region. On T1-weighted imaging (T1WI), the lesion demonstrated an isointense or hypointense central area with peripheral hyperintensity. On T2-weighted imaging (T2WI), it exhibited mixed isointense or high signals with internal linear hypointense strands. The tumor showed an isointense signal on diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and demonstrated marked enhancement on contrast-enhanced MRI. The patient underwent surgical resection, and histopathologic examination confirmed ELST. Conclusions: When imaging of a mass in the cerebellopontine angle reveals a high-signal intensity surrounding the lesion on T1WI, physicians should be highly vigilant for ELST.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 4(2):e0058, June 2026. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000058

item.author: Wilkins, Gregory N.; Rynkowsky, Edward; DeLatour, Frank; McLaughlin, Sloan M.; Handel, Steven E.; Sabol, Jennifer V.
item.title: Restoring a Severely Atrophic Maxilla with a Subperiosteal Implant: A Case Report
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Fri, 20 Mar 2026 00:00:00 GMT-05:00

Edentulous patients not amenable to dental implants due to a severely atrophic ridge may not have suitably retentive dental prostheses. Subperiosteal dental implants, once discarded as a viable treatment option due to postoperative infection and subsequent failure, have become a viable treatment option once again due to newer surgical techniques, technology, and biomaterials. A 54-year-old male presented with a chief complaint of an ill-fitting maxillary complete denture and inability to masticate efficiently. Clinical and radiographic examination revealed severe atrophy of the maxilla with pneumatized sinuses and was not a candidate for traditional dental implants or bone augmentation. A new maxillary complete denture was fabricated and used as a scan appliance and fixed prosthesis template to design a subperiosteal implant. A cone beam computed tomography (CBCT) scan and Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) file were obtained and the information was sent to KLS-Martin (Tuttlingen, Germany) for virtual planning. Once approved by the surgical and restorative teams, the titanium subperiosteal implant and prosthesis prototype were fabricated and delivered under general anesthesia. After 6 months of wearing the PMMA prototype, a definitive zirconia prosthesis was delivered.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 4(2):e0056, June 2026. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000056

item.author: Stroud, Anna; Van Gelder, James; Boughton, Phillip
item.title: Integrated Approach to Evacuation of Chronic Subdural Hematoma: Slotted Burr Hole Access and a Novel Bioactive Scaffold for Cranial Defect Closure
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Fri, 13 Mar 2026 00:00:00 GMT-05:00

Burr hole craniotomy is the most common approach to chronic subdural hematoma (cSDH) evacuation, yet it leaves a cranial defect that can cause cosmetically significant scalp depression. Drain placement remains highly variable and is associated with migration, obstruction, and potential iatrogenic injury. Modified burr hole geometry and purpose-built closure devices may improve operative access, postoperative outcomes, and patient satisfaction. A systematic review of closure techniques for burr hole defects was performed. In silico and in vitro skull models compared standard burr holes, slotted burr holes, and 30-mm craniotomies in frontal, temporoparietal, and occipital locations. Actv-graft® was subjected to deflection testing, drainage simulation using a saline–carrageenan mixture, and drain dislodgement testing using a load sensor. The literature suggests that burr hole closure reduces visible skin depression without increased complications. Slotted burr holes provided greater surface visualization compared with conventional burr holes, with statistically significant improvement in in vitro simulations. Actv-graft® tolerated 21.9 N of load without dislodgement, demonstrated complete shape recovery, increased drainage volume before occlusion (17.5 versus 1.9 mL; P<0.01), and allowed safe drain removal with a low mean pulling force of 2.12 N. A slotted burr hole technique paired with the use of an Actv-graft® bioscaffold may enhance surgical exposure, reduce cosmetic deformity, improve drainage reliability, and permit safer drain stabilization. These findings support further in vivo evaluation of this integrated approach to burr hole management.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 4(1):e0053, March 2026. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000053

item.author: Ambrosio, Eloá Cristina Passucci; Dourado, Ana Gabriela Gurgel; da Silveira, Ana Beatriz Vieira; Mello-Peixoto, Yana Cosendey Toledo; Soares, Simone; Nalabothu, Prasad; Carrara, Cleide Felício Carvalho; Machado, Maria Aparecida Andrade Moreira; Oliveira, Thais Marchini
item.title: 3D Stereophotogrammetry Analysis of Palatal Area and Dental Occlusion in Children With and Without Oral Clefts
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 00:00:00 GMT-06:00

This study aimed to analyze the area of dental arches and occlusion of children with and without cleft lip and palate at 5 years of age. Methods: Group 1 (unilateral cleft lip and palate) and group 2 (without cleft lip and palate) comprised 30 children each. The palatal surface area was assessed by marking points in a clockwise direction along the gingival margin of the teeth and drawing a straight line between the distal-palatal points of the deciduous second molars. Occlusion was also evaluated using the Atack index. Both the palatal surface and Atack index were assessed using digitized dental models. 3D analyses of the dental arches were performed using the stereophotogrammetry system software. Intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC), Shapiro-Wilk normality test, and Mann-Whitney test were applied (α=5%). Group 1 showed a significantly lower value in the area analysis (P=0.002). Atack index 4 was the most frequent (n=8, 27%), followed by index 1, 2, 5, and 3 (n=7, 23%; n=6, 20%; n=6, 20%; n=3, 10%, respectively). It is concluded that children with cleft lip and palate had a reduced palatal surface area compared with children without craniofacial anomalies at 5 years of age. Most children with orofacial clefts had unfavorable occlusal indices due to negative horizontal overjet and crossbite.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 4(1):e0049, March 2026. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000049

item.author: Sydorchuk, Olena; Yi, Kyu‑Ho
item.title: Hair Extensions: Anatomic, Biological, and Clinical Implications in Alopecia
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Mon, 02 Feb 2026 00:00:00 GMT-06:00

Background: Hair extensions are widely used for cosmetic and medical reasons, but durable attachment systems (thermopolymer, adhesive, mechanical, and temporary) can impose mechanical, chemical, and thermal stresses that disrupt scalp biology and precipitate hair loss. Objective: To synthesize anatomic, biological, and clinical evidence on hair-extension practices with emphasis on alopecia risk, diagnosis, prevention, and management. Methods: Narrative review (1990–2025) across dermatology and trichology literature covering biomechanics, barrier/microbiome, thermal injury, phenotypes, diagnostic imaging, prevention, and therapies. Results: Sustained traction remodels the perifollicular matrix, compromises microcirculation, shortens anagen, and promotes miniaturization with frontal/temporal predilection. Adhesives and removers can disrupt the stratum corneum and trigger irritant or allergic contact dermatitis. Occlusion elevates humidity/temperature, shifts pH/TEWL, and fosters dysbiosis. Early load reduction with anti-inflammatory and barrier-repair care reverses most early disease; advanced cases show perifollicular fibrosis with limited reversibility. Standardized wear cycles, anchor-load distribution, patch testing, hygiene/ventilation, and stylist–clinician collaboration reduce risk. Regenerative adjuncts (PRP and low-level laser) have supportive evidence in nonscarring alopecias; exosomes remain investigational. Conclusions: Extensions confer psychosocial benefit yet impose nontrivial biological costs. A prevention-first, medical model—integrating risk stratification, standardized technique, early surveillance, and tiered therapy—can minimize iatrogenic alopecia and maintain scalp health.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 4(1):e0051, March 2026. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000051

item.author: Yatsunenko, Sergey; Latsunenko, Anatolii; Oliynyk, Sergiy; Yi, Kyu-Ho
item.title: Biophysical Mechanisms and Clinical Potential of Millimeter-Wave Therapy in Aesthetic Medicine
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Mon, 15 Dec 2025 00:00:00 GMT-06:00

Electromagnetic waves in the extremely high frequency (EHF, 30–300 GHz) range represent a non-thermal physical factor with pronounced bioregulatory effects. Their interaction with cellular structures is predominantly informational in nature and is realized through modulation of membrane protein activity, ion channels, and water structuring. These effects find practical application in cosmetology, where the key objectives are the deceleration of age-related skin changes and the acceleration of regeneration following aesthetic procedures. This article considers 2 principal directions for the use of EHF irradiation: slowing the aging of the facial and neck skin by stimulating proliferation of keratinocytes and fibroblasts, activating collagen synthesis and antioxidant mechanisms; accelerating wound healing after plastic surgery, including shortened epithelialization time, reduced inflammatory response, and minimized risk of hypertrophic scarring. Experimental and clinical data are presented, demonstrating the effects of EHF exposure on cellular renewal, microcirculation, and tissue regeneration. Particular attention is given to the prospects of integrating EHF therapy into comprehensive cosmetology protocols, including its combination with laser, phototherapeutic, and injectable methods. Thus, EHF irradiation is viewed as a promising tool in aesthetic medicine, capable of enhancing the efficacy of cosmetic procedures, expediting recovery after surgical interventions, and contributing to a more “natural” external result.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 3(4):e0052, December 2025. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000052

item.author: Namgung, Sara; Lee, Jeonghee; Yi, Kyu-Ho
item.title: Human Serum Albumin and Dermal BoNT: A Safety Consideration
item.description:
所属期数: Ahead of Print
出版时间: Wed, 03 Dec 2025 00:00:00 GMT-06:00

Background: HSA is widely used as a stabilizer in BoNT formulations and is well tolerated for labeled IM uses. The adoption of intradermal (“dermatoxin,” micro-BoNT) techniques raises a safety question: does placing an HSA-containing injectate directly into the immune-dense dermis elevate the risk of cutaneous hypersensitivity? Innovation/Idea: The authors propose reframing excipient choice as a route-specific safety variable and call for the development and clinical study of HSA-free BoNT formulations (TYEMVERS, JKD Bio, Seoul, Korea) for intradermal applications. Rationale: The dermis contains mast cells, Langerhans cells, dendritic cells, tissue-resident T cells, and robust lymphatic access, promoting rapid antigen sampling. Published reports document HSA-related hypersensitivity ranging from localized reactions to rare anaphylaxis. Given heightened cutaneous immune surveillance, ID administration may increase the likelihood or visibility of excipient-triggered reactions. Implications: A pragmatic evaluation pathway—comparative safety trials, mechanistic substudies, and improved pharmacovigilance—can test this hypothesis. Meanwhile, risk-aware clinical practice (patient selection, counseling, and monitoring) may reduce adverse events while preserving aesthetic outcomes.
item.citation: Journal of Craniofacial Surgery Open. 3(4):e0050, December 2025. doi: 10.1097/SC9.0000000000000050
